description:
Your role: you are an edge-computing engineer who must prove that the firmware-update
    API you will soon deploy to thousands of IoT devices is behaving correctly.
    Do *everything* from the command line inside the Linux terminal (no GUI
    editors, no IDEs).

    Objective
    ----------
    1.   Stand-up a **very small local mock HTTP server** that listens on TCP port
         9000 and exposes two endpoints:
         •  GET  /device/health   → must answer HTTP 200 with body
            {"device":"edge-node-1","status":"healthy"}
         •  POST /device/update   → must answer HTTP 202 with body
            {"update":"received","device":"edge-node-1"}

    2.   Using only     curl     (no Python-requests, wget, etc.)
         a) Perform a GET request to /device/health  
         b) Perform a POST request to /device/update where the request-body you
            send is the literal text “update=start” (any Content-Type you like)
         c) Perform a HEAD request to /device/health and capture the returned
            HTTP status code only (no body)

    3.   Capture the terminal-visible responses from all three curl calls in a
         single log file named
                /home/user/iot_edge/api_test/api_responses.log

         The log file **must follow EXACTLY** the template below so that the
         automated grader can parse it:

                GET_RESPONSE: {"device":"edge-node-1","status":"healthy"}
                POST_RESPONSE: {"update":"received","device":"edge-node-1"}
                HEAD_STATUS: 200

         •  One newline between each line, no leading/trailing blank lines
         •  The JSON must be minified (one single line, no spaces except the one
            after each colon shown above)
         •  Use the same letter-case as shown, including the capitalised keys

    4.   All project artefacts must live in
                /home/user/iot_edge/api_test
         That directory must contain, at a minimum:
         •  server.py   (your mock API implementation)
         •  api_responses.log  (described above)

    5.   The server can keep running or be stopped when you finish; the grader
         only inspects the filesystem.

    Hints (do NOT copy-paste; they’re just guidance, not commands)
    --------------------------------------------------------------
    •  You can write a tiny HTTP server in ≈15 lines of Python using
         from http.server import BaseHTTPRequestHandler, HTTPServer
    •  To run it in the background, shell ‘&’ suffices; remember to
         redirect stdout/stderr somewhere if you don’t want them cluttering
    •  curl’s   -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}"   is handy for HEAD requests

    Success criteria recap
    ----------------------
    1. /home/user/iot_edge/api_test exists
    2. /home/user/iot_edge/api_test/server.py implements the two endpoints exactly
    3. /home/user/iot_edge/api_test/api_responses.log exists and matches the
       three-line template verbatim
truth:
Directory layout after completion
    ---------------------------------
    /home/user/iot_edge/api_test/
        ├── server.py
        └── api_responses.log

    api_responses.log exact contents
    --------------------------------
    GET_RESPONSE: {"device":"edge-node-1","status":"healthy"}
    POST_RESPONSE: {"update":"received","device":"edge-node-1"}
    HEAD_STATUS: 200

    (Newline at the end of the last line is OPTIONAL.)

    server.py minimal behavioural contract
    --------------------------------------
      • Listens on 0.0.0.0:9000
      • GET  /device/health → HTTP 200, body exactly
            {"device":"edge-node-1","status":"healthy"}
      • POST /device/update → HTTP 202, body exactly
            {"update":"received","device":"edge-node-1"}
      • Any other method/route returns 404 or 501 (grader ignores these)

    The grader will:
      1. Check that /home/user/iot_edge/api_test/api_responses.log exists
      2. Read the file and compare it byte-for-byte with the block above
      3. Optionally import server.py, perform the two calls, and ensure the
         returned payloads/status codes match the behavioural contract
